speaker: interviewer
text: --16, 1996, with survivor Raymonde Fiol, interviewed by Wendy Kreeger. Did you often go to, to look at the POW camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I used to--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it located?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just down the road and up the hill. I recall that very distinctly. And there was a camp, obviously surrounded by barbed wire. And I remember the prisoners. I remember the initials, POW. Of course, they didn't mean anything to me. I thought it was a French word of some kind. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It was on a sign outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they wore-- the prisoners had on the-- their uniforms. Whatever they wore had POW on it-- I recall that-- of course, guarded by the Americans. And I would just go in there and watch them. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever talk to the Americans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they would come in the cafe. And I was the--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like? Was that a difference?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nice, yes, very nice. And I was a little puzzled because there was an American soldier. He was a black man. And I had never seen a black person in my life. So I was a little intrigued. And he was very nice, I recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ask someone about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I, I thought it was funny because I thought he was made up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was-- that's just, just the color of the skin. And I guess I had to accept that, you know. [LAUGHS] But it was-- it was just very intriguing to a little child, having never seen a black man.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was also, I recall, a French man. And he was in the army. And I said, how could you be French and be in the American Army? Puzzling also-- I remember that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they would come in and give me all kinds of chocolates. I don't think I recall having had chocolates as a child. And I remember that very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: You liked the chocolates?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very much. [LAUGHS] They would bring me all kinds of treats. Yeah, they were very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the atmosphere different in the cafe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- it was-- almost felt like it was no longer a cafe, more like a, a big family, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the same Americans come in every night?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure they were-- yeah, mostly probably officers would come in and just sit and talk and inquire, you know, just asked us questions and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see a change in the Cailacs' behavior?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they like now?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] More relaxed. Not only that, Evelyn stopped going out at night. [LAUGHS] She didn't have to do that anymore. And it was just totally different. I also stopped going to catechism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, oh yes, definitely. But I didn't have to go to church anymore. And I had to-- I didn't have to go to catechism. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember being told that. I wasn't very happy in the first place, anyway. And I really was always a very good student. But I never learned anything anyway. It went in one ear and out the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they explain why you didn't have to go anymore?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because the war was over. And I didn't have to. Don't forget, they were Protestant. So there was no reason for them to make me go to a Catholic church.

speaker: interviewer
text: And pretend to be not Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, yes. Yes. So that was a relief. And there was definitely a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] different atmosphere. You felt free. The bombing stopped. The leaflets stopped falling from the sky. And the Germans were gone. Other than-- and I think that's why I was totally delighted to see them behind the fences. It was their turn. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah, it became a, a more relaxed time. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] it was the end. I remember cheering in the streets, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel Jewish at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't feel anything. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't think there was any time in my life that I was immersed in Judaism per se. Only why was I different?

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you know why you had gone through what you did because you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't understand it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you knew the fact of it? You had--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --been told?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, oh yes, from that time when the German had asked my mother, you know, why? [CRYING] And I guess, at that time, I must have asked my mother, why do I have to wear a star? And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she had to give me, you know, the reason, that we were Jewish and we're held apart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yet your family had been very assimilated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not so much assimilated-- no, I don't think it's-- you can call it assimilated. I think you call it-- what would you say that they were free of practicing religion? They were not-- they did not consider themselves assimilated. I think, if anything, the, the Yiddishkeit was there at all times. There was-- Yiddish was my first language. I had to learn French, even though I was French born. And no, it was just a total escape from structured religion. But in no way, I don't think they were assimilated. I think their friends, everybody, was Jewish. There was no assimilation then, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just couldn't understand why we would be ostracized. And how do you explain to a child? So I had no understanding of that. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But I knew my identity. [CRYING] I did, yeah. So you went from this-- the safer existence with the Cailacs--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to the upset of being sent to the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And meeting your aunt and uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of child do you think you were at the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Withdrawn, very shy, very reserved-- I think that's what I-- you know, I became that-- and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] having tantrums, later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was at the orphanage? Or that--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was with the aunt and uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, uh-huh, crying fits. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [CRYING] not knowing where I stood-- [CRYING] [SOBBING] feeling safe in one place and being taken away. And being sent here and sent there. So I didn't want it-- I don't-- I didn't want it to happen. [CRYING] [SOBBING] So I was unhappy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you grow to feel close to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: To my uncle, yes. My aunt, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you moved from your parent's apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: To where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To another apartment in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. That's 7, Cite Voltaire. It was a different neighborhood. It wasn't a very big apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was smaller than your old apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, much smaller. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had to sleep in the living room. So-- but I went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is yet a different school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, then there was a school in Paris, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It's called Cite Voltaire Primary Grade. And I would go. After that, I was sent [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to my cousin's wedding in London-- my cousin Stella. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were very fond of her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was her stepmother. But it was her father. And I remember I went in the wintertime. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was, I guess at that point, 1947. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I stayed just a week or so for the wedding. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had to go back to Paris. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And from then on, I was sent every year to London to spend my summer vacations, first with my uncle until he died, and then with my cousin Stella afterwards, until I was 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were those happy times--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the visits to London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Better than living in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, [CRYING] very much, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the orphanage, were there are other Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were all Jewish children. They were all the children that had been found or-- I believe it was run by ORT. Yes, definitely-- was definitely all Jewish children who were all orphans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you share stories with them--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --about the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, [CRYING] because I guess [SOBBING] we all had our little stories, and-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make friends with anyone at the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Just-- you know, I think we were all a bit traumatized. [CRYING] There were a lot of-- a, you know, big span of-- you know, from younger than me-- or just about my age-- to teenagers, until homes were found for all of us, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what sort of guardians were your aunt and uncle in Paris? Were they strict? Were they kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my aunt had been traumatized by the war and, I think, had lost a, a child in infancy, and I don't think was very accepting of me. [CRYING] So it was a little hard. [SOBBING] My uncle was very kind. But it was hard for me, very hard. I couldn't-- always felt somewhat of a stranger in their home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember revisiting places that you had seen when you were a little child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't go. I went back to Andresy only after I'd married, to show my husband. I didn't go back. I've never gone back--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --to the Ardennes. Andresy was the orphanage. I never went back to the Ardennes at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were living with your aunt and uncle in Paris--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you come upon landmarks that you remembered from your earlier life in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Not really. I don't think our movements were very free when we were in Paris. No, I did not really recall anything. I actually recall being on vacation at the beach with my parents. But not--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, yeah, Les Sables de-- Les Sables d'Olonne. It's on the west coast, obviously, of France, on the Mediterranean-- on the Atlantic side, yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But now I have the feeling that probably our movements in Paris were pretty restricted, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are things still in the apartment-- your parents' apartment, when you went there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, the furnishings-- the furniture-- there was still furniture. I couldn't say whether things had been stolen, which maybe they were. But basically, the apartment was in-- never lived in by anyone from the time my parents had left it. So I guess the authorities left it intact.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were some personal effects?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe so. But my aunt and uncle took everything because they were-- they didn't have anything. So it gave them a chance to give them a start. Because they came out of Italy with absolutely nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about it at the time? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Resentful. It was mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever say so?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk much about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [CRYING] Nobody cared to talk about them, so no. [CRYING] Once in awhile I would ask my uncle. And he would just give me a brief answer. [SOBBING] So no. [CRYING] And my aunt didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So actually it was more my cousin, Stella, who would tell me about my father. Because she had lost her mother before the war. She was quite a-- she was 15 years older than me. So he would take her to France as a little girl on vacation. And she was happy to do the same for me, [CRYING] yeah. And so she had real memories of your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, absolutely. Yeah, yes. And of course, her father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she remember your mother also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I believe after they married, he stopped sending for her, [LAUGHS] yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you pass through the tantrum and the crying stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, eventually I did, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to school in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I went to school, primary grade first. And then I went on to high school, high school combination college. And I was skipped two years. I skipped two grades. So I went on at 17-- 16-- I went to London and went to college for a year and a half there, for two years. And I stayed with my cousin Stella at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you studying? Just-- Just--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to business. I studied business. And at 18-- 17 and 1/2, 18-- I went back to Paris, and went back to my aunt and uncle, and started to go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went into secretarial-- secretary. And because I was bilingual, French and English, I got a bilingual position immediately. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went to-- I used to belong to a choir run by a youth organization from a, a-- Jewish. And they had organized what they called a Zimriyah for-- to attract young people in Israel. And I had to pay a minimal fee with my whole group to go to Israel. So we were treated in 1955 to a very nice trip hosted by the Israeli government to try and attract settlers and had a lovely time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- you went with your choir?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were singing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you tempted to settle there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I think I had enough disruptions in my life. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember being aware of that, that you couldn't make another change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, oh yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have friends from the group that stayed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they sort of cheated. We had students from the opera join the choir. [LAUGHS] And they were not Jewish. And they stayed and converted. So I think it served a purpose. Yes, they were totally overwhelmed by what they-- I mean it was just incredible seeing little trees like this growing in the desert. And I finally met my Uncle Jakob that had been in Palestine all along. It was my father's oldest brother. Just-- I met him briefly. He'd didn't have any children. So I didn't have any cousins to meet. And when I came back, I met my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I meet my husband? It was Rosh Hashanah. And two friends of mine were walking around. And we went-- we had just went to see if the Jewish Center where we belonged to as singers was open. It wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the director lived upstairs. And we rang the bell. Anyway, he said, well, you know, oh how nice. I have two Americans here. Would you like to [LAUGHS] meet them? One of them was my husband. [LAUGHS] So we met on Rosh Hashanah. And our first date was on Yom Kippur. [LAUGHS] Now were you and your aunt and uncle at this point observant--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you did belong to this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Jewish organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I said, yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- over the years, I was taught Yiddish, how to write. I went and got some--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was post-war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I got Yiddish education, more than towards the Hebrew. And, like I said, my uncle was from the same school as my father. My, my uncle was an ordained rabbi. And it was a total escape from religion, which came back to him as he grew older. But he would--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: My Uncle Gabriel in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who you were living with.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, who I was living with-- had the same feelings.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had been an ordained--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: --before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he had given it up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he had, yeah. But while I was in England with my cousin Stella and my-- my Uncle Leon was the same thing in London, not religious. But his daughter, Stella, really became religious. And I got a good basis there from the summers and going to temple, and, you know, having Shabbos, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] seeing Jewish religion and going to-- being observant. And usually I was there for the holidays. I'd go to temple. I was forced to fast. But it's fine. [LAUGHS] When I turned 13 they said, you had to fast. I said, OK. And so I got my Jewish education, basically, more from my cousin in London than from any of my two uncles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the relations between the London relatives and the Paris relatives good?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had worked out the system of the summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --summers in London.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, definitely, yes. That was-- I, I mean, from 11 on-- I-- no, 12 on-- I was going to England by myself from Paris. I'd be on the train in Paris and get off the train, get on the boat, cross the channel, get back on the train to go to London. And they were waiting for me at the station, yes. And same back in-- same in reverse, yes. Yeah. So I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now tell us about your husband. His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Philip Fiol. And like I said, we met on Rosh Hashanah. And it was a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] quick courtship, I guess, in today's--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he doing in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in the army. He had been stationed in Germany and moved to France. And because it was Rosh Hashanah, he was looking for a Jewish environment. He felt, I guess, homesick. He and his friend, they were both Jewish. I think they had given them in their little booklets from the army where to find Jewish houses of worship and stuff. So we met on Rosh Hashanah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he turned up at the same place that you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1955, yeah. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went out with his friend. [LAUGHS] He went out with my girlfriend. And I believe he asked his-- my girlfriend, are you-- or his friend, are you interested in her? He said, no, not really. He says, can I go with her? [LAUGHS] So this is how we started our courtship. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh yeah, my cousin in England, Stella, had-- was on the verge of convincing my aunt and my Uncle Gabriel from Paris to move to England. Because she really wanted me there, too. And she felt it was a way to move all of us to England. And we were in the process of doing that. But, of course, when I told them that I had met a young man, I called them up and said, I'm going to be marrying an American. They said, don't do anything. We'll be here next weekend. [LAUGHS] They came to Paris, tried to stop it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stella did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did. I believe they offered him money. Well, that's all they had to do. [LAUGHS] And we were married the next October. I guess they realized they couldn't do anything. They actually even offered the two of us to move to England, which we promptly refused, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: He wanted to come back here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Possibly, but the fact that they hadn't done a great impression in the beginning with him wasn't too convincing at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about it? Were you torn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was torn. But I really went along with him because I would-- probably had-- you know, I was just as insulted at the time. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe yourself in your-- at this point in your life, just before you met him and when you met him?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, before, you had gone through the different phases. And you went through the tantrums with-- you were now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Happy. I was happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were happy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Feeling safe--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in control of my life, first time, [CRYING] where I could dictate how I want to live my life, yeah. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And that continued when you met him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah. And so in '57-- six months, I guess, later, until all the paperwork for the Army were completed for me-- we came to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Interesting experience. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You flew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because it was the Hungarian Revolution. And all the refugees were being flown to the United States. So we had to come by boat. And I crossed the ocean [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] being seasick for 14 days. You want to lose weight? That's the way to do it. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your actual arrival?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. Yes, it was in April 7, 1957, Fort Hamilton, Brooklyn, New York. [LAUGHS] I think-- my first step into New York, and I'm in-- you know, in the United States-- and I land in Brooklyn.
